rationale: The citing paper explicitly frames itself as extending prior suspense/surprise engagement research and focuses on surprise/suspense effects on retention (leave decisions), while the cited 2012 JMR paper directly studies surprise-driven engagement via attention and zapping (retention) in internet video ads. Given this strong construct and outcome overlap (surprise, attention/engagement, retention behavior), the citation is plausibly foundational/substantive rather than incidental. With only DOI-level match but high topical alignment, this pair should be kept for full relation judgment.
